title: Technology Briefing: Best Stocks to Buy in Security, Identity & Data Centers for AI Agents
summary: An analysis of the current landscape for security, identity and data center stocks, focusing on platform shifts toward AI, regulatory pressures, tax incentives, and community concerns that shape investment prospects.
imageAlt: Image from Investing.com for Best Stocks to Buy in Security, Identity & Data Centers for AI Agents - Investing.com
imageCaption: Best Stocks to Buy in Security, Identity & Data Centers for AI Agents - Investing.com (Investing.com)

body.text: The surge in AI agent deployment has accelerated demand for specialized infrastructure that blends high‑performance compute with robust security and identity controls. Investors are now looking beyond traditional cloud providers to companies that can deliver these capabilities at scale.

Investing.com’s recent coverage highlights a cohort of security, identity and data center firms that are positioning themselves as the backbone for AI workloads. These companies are investing in purpose‑built hardware, edge‑to‑cloud architectures, and software‑defined networking that reduce latency and improve throughput for large language models and real‑time inference. The shift is driven by the need for deterministic performance and the integration of AI‑native security features such as hardware attestation and secure enclaves.

Security and identity solutions are becoming critical touchpoints in AI pipelines. Zero‑trust frameworks, continuous authentication, and fine‑grained access controls are no longer optional; they are prerequisites for compliance and risk mitigation. Companies that embed these capabilities directly into their data center offerings can command premium pricing and lock in long‑term contracts with enterprise AI developers. The convergence of security and infrastructure is a key differentiator in a market where performance alone is insufficient.

The push for larger facilities is not without friction. In Springfield, Ohio, residents have organized to ban new mega‑data centers, citing concerns over traffic, power consumption, and environmental impact. This local opposition reflects a broader trend where community pushback can delay or derail projects, adding uncertainty to capital expenditures. Investors must factor in the risk of regulatory delays and the potential need for additional permitting or mitigation strategies.

Ohio’s recent data center tax break, intended to spur growth, has proven more expensive than projected, with a $1.4 billion shortfall expected in 2025. The discrepancy underscores the volatility of state‑level incentives and the importance of scrutinizing the long‑term fiscal sustainability of such programs. Companies that rely heavily on tax credits may face margin compression if state budgets tighten or if political priorities shift.

Beyond local opposition, data centers can pose environmental risks. A Georgia data center has been accused of contaminating a county’s drinking water supply, raising questions about water usage, waste management, and compliance with environmental regulations. Such incidents can lead to costly remediation, legal liability, and reputational damage—all factors that influence the risk profile of a data center operator.

items: Reliability of tax incentives and state support

body.text: The intersection of AI demand, security imperatives, and regulatory scrutiny is reshaping the data center landscape. Companies that can navigate community concerns, secure reliable tax incentives, and embed advanced security into their infrastructure stand to capture a growing share of the AI‑driven market. As the technology ecosystem evolves, so too will the criteria that differentiate the leaders from the laggards.
